description: An electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) boundary map of an Fe–8% Al low-density steel investigated under hot-torsion deformation conditions. Black lines represent high-angle grain boundaries (HABs, ≥15° misorientation) and red lines represent low-angle boundaries (LABs, 2°–15°), as defined in the paper’s EBSD methodology. The map includes a 500 μm scale bar and directional arrows indicating the shear axes. The microstructure exhibits large elongated ferrite grains extensively segmented by red LABs, together with finer grain domains, reflecting dynamic microstructural evolution during high-temperature deformation. Depending on the specific processing window, this morphology corresponds either to progressive subgrain involution characteristic of continuous dynamic recrystallization (CDRX) or to a bimodal mixture of recrystallized and deformed grains associated with discontinuous dynamic recrystallization (DDRX). The figure thereby illustrates how hot-working parameters control boundary character distribution and grain fragmentation, underpinning the study’s central thesis that CDRX versus DDRX dominance can be tuned to tailor crystallographic texture and roping resistance in ferritic low-density steels.
